title: How DAG scheduling and execution dates work
description: Why orchestrators run a logical/execution date per interval and how that date parameterizes idempotent tasks.
body: Use when reasoning about when and for what date a DAG run executes. Orchestrators schedule one run per interval, each tagged with a logical/execution date representing the data window it covers, and tasks should derive their inputs and outputs from that date. Do not use the wall-clock 'now' inside a task — backfills and retries then process the wrong window. Actions: reference the execution date for partitions and queries so any run is reproducible. Validation signals include a backfilled run for a past date producing that date's output and a rerun reproducing it. Risk boundary: using wall-clock time breaks backfills silently; if a task reads 'now', treat it as non-reproducible and escalate to parameterize it by execution date.
